patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious grocery shift supervisor. Speak in a tangential, worried way and jump between symptoms and work stress. Start with: on-and-off chest tightness and getting out of breath faster than before, mostly when walking fast or climbing stairs, plus unusual fatigue for 2 months. Volunteer that you think it’s anxiety/panic, heartburn/acid reflux, or a pulled muscle because it comes and goes.

If asked about associated symptoms, share palpitations (racing/fluttering minutes), lightheadedness esp. standing quickly, ankle puffiness by evening, waking at night short of breath needing to sit up, needing more pillows, 6–8 lb weight gain, and a dry nighttime cough.

Do not mention unless directly asked: heavy ibuprofen use, worse shortness of breath lying flat, shoes tighter, loud snoring, unrefreshed sleep, morning headaches.

Resist admitting cocaine use, increased alcohol to sleep, and not taking BP meds; minimize at first (“not much/only sometimes”), then disclose if asked nonjudgmentally.

Emphasize worry about missing work and surprise bills; push for “just a medication.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share more, and ask if this could be serious.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute uncomplicated cystitis (urinary tract infection)
required_evidence: Acute onset dysuria, urinary frequency/urgency, or suprapubic discomfort without vaginal discharge/irritation
No systemic features suggesting pyelonephritis (e.g., fever, flank pain, rigors, vomiting)
Urinalysis supportive of UTI (positive leukocyte esterase and/or nitrites, pyuria) if obtained
No signs of complicated infection (e.g., pregnancy, urinary obstruction, recent instrumentation, immunocompromise) or these are explicitly assessed

expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal or mildly elevated; marked leukocytosis suggests upper tract or systemic infection
expected_lab_results.BUN: Typically normal (elevation may suggest dehydration or renal impairment impacting antibiotic choice)

medications: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100 mg PO BID x 5 days
Phenazopyridine 200 mg PO TID after meals x 2 days (optional for dysuria)
non_pharmacological: Encourage oral hydration as tolerated
Avoid bladder irritants (caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods) during symptoms
Return precautions for fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, vomiting, or no improvement within 48–72 hours
Consider urine culture if recurrent infection, recent antibiotics, atypical symptoms, or treatment failure
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48–72 hours if not improving; sooner/emergent evaluation if systemic symptoms develop

must_include: Likely bladder infection (lower UTI) based on symptoms and absence of kidney infection signs
What symptoms would suggest progression to kidney infection (fever, flank pain, vomiting)
Antibiotic name, dose, duration, and importance of completing the course
Expected timeline of symptom improvement (often within 24–48 hours)
Phenazopyridine is optional, short-term only, and can discolor urine
Review penicillin allergy and reassure chosen regimen avoids beta-lactams
Discuss common side effects and when to stop/seek care (rash, breathing difficulty, severe diarrhea)
Hydration and avoidance of bladder irritants
Prevention counseling if relevant (e.g., urinate after sex, avoid spermicides if recurrent)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key urinary symptoms, duration, pregnancy status, sexual activity, prior UTIs, comorbidities, and red flags for pyelonephritis or STI to determine if this is uncomplicated cystitis vs complicated infection.
Obtain urinalysis (and urine culture if indicated) and pregnancy test when relevant to support diagnosis and guide antibiotic choice.

Review UA/culture (if ordered) and pregnancy results to confirm likely lower UTI and identify findings that would change management (e.g., nitrites/LE, hematuria, contaminated sample, resistant organism).

State likely diagnosis (lower UTI/cystitis) and explicitly note absence/presence of kidney infection signs; consider differentials (vaginitis/STI, urethritis, interstitial cystitis) if symptoms atypical.
Critical safety step before prescribing: confirm penicillin allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing) and other medication allergies to avoid unsafe antibiotics.
Non-critical but expected safety step: screen for interactions with candidate UTI antibiotics (e.g., TMP-SMX with warfarin; nitrofurantoin with certain conditions/agents) and current meds.

Critical: perform contraindication check before selecting and issuing an antibiotic and any adjunct (e.g., phenazopyridine). Choose guideline-concordant regimen and document dose/duration; avoid beta-lactams if penicillin allergy concern and alternatives appropriate.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (including expected improvement timeline), optional phenazopyridine counseling, side effects/return precautions, hydration/irritant avoidance, and prevention advice if relevant.
Arrange follow-up or safety-net: re-evaluation if not improving within 48–72 hours, sooner if red flags; culture follow-up if obtained or if recurrent/complicated.
Close the encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and ensuring clear next steps.

Start with: “My joints keep swelling up and hurting, and it’s making it hard to get around.” Volunteer early: knees/ankles get puffy and sore on and off, morning stiffness that takes a while to loosen, lots of fatigue even with sleep, sometimes limp because one leg hurts more. Express misconceptions: think it might be growing pains, a sports injury, or lingering infection after sore throats. diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) associated with LACC1 deficiency (monogenic early-onset inflammatory arthritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 mutation
required_evidence: Chronic inflammatory arthritis with symptom duration >6 weeks in a child/adolescent
Objective joint inflammation (swelling, warmth, or limited range of motion with pain) in ≥1 joint
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) consistent with active inflammation
RF negative and anti-CCP negative (supports seronegative JIA phenotype)
Known LACC1 pathogenic variant or prior genetic diagnosis consistent with LACC1-associated juvenile arthritis

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly low (anemia of chronic inflammation possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)

medications: Meloxicam 0.125 mg/kg PO once daily (max 7.5 mg/day) with food
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m2 PO or SC once weekly (max 25 mg/week)
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg PO once weekly, not on methotrexate day)
Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for short course as bridge therapy if significant flare/function-limiting symptoms (taper as soon as controlled)
non_pharmacological: Referral to pediatric rheumatology (if not already established) and shared decision-making on DMARD/biologic escalation based on activity
Physical therapy/occupational therapy for range-of-motion, strengthening, and function; home exercise plan
Activity as tolerated; joint protection strategies; school accommodations as needed
Heat/cold modalities for symptom relief
Screen for uveitis risk and arrange ophthalmology slit-lamp screening per JIA schedule
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for symptom control and inflammatory markers; earlier (1-2 weeks) if worsening pain, fever, GI bleeding symptoms, or medication adverse effects

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood; LACC1 is a genetic cause that can drive inflammation
Symptoms can fluctuate (flares/remissions) and untreated inflammation can damage joints and affect growth/function
Blood tests (ESR/CRP) reflect inflammation; RF/anti-CCP are often negative in this condition
NSAID (meloxicam) helps pain/stiffness but may not fully control disease; DMARD (methotrexate) treats underlying inflammation and prevents damage
Methotrexate is taken weekly (not daily) and folic acid reduces side effects
Expected time to benefit for methotrexate is typically 6-12 weeks; bridging steroids may be short-term only

Key adverse effects to watch: NSAID stomach pain/bleeding and kidney issues; methotrexate nausea, mouth sores, liver irritation, low blood counts; seek care for fever, severe sore throat, easy bruising/bleeding
Review allergies: penicillin caused urticarial rash; latex causes contact dermatitis—avoid latex-containing supplies
Discuss pregnancy prevention/teratogenicity counseling for methotrexate when age-appropriate; avoid alcohol and discuss vaccination planning
Create a plan for pain control, school participation, and when to contact the clinic/ER
Maintain regular low-impact activity and stretching to preserve joint motion
Use heat in morning stiffness and ice after activity for swelling
Adherence to weekly dosing schedule and follow-up labs/visits is essential
Importance of eye screening for uveitis even without eye symptoms

Order baseline and activity labs for juvenile inflammatory arthritis and DMARD safety: ESR/CRP, CBC with differential, CMP (AST/ALT, creatinine), +/- hepatitis B/C and TB screening if escalation to biologic is considered; pregnancy test when age-appropriate before methotrexate.
Non-critical pre-prescribing check: screen for interactions (e.g., methotrexate with TMP-SMX; NSAIDs with renal risk/other nephrotoxins) and adjust plan if present.

State diagnosis consistent with juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect and explain expected lab patterns (ESR/CRP elevated; RF/anti-CCP often negative).
Initiate guideline-consistent therapy: NSAID for symptom relief and methotrexate as DMARD with weekly dosing and folic acid; consider short-term bridging steroids only if needed.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale/timeline, adverse effects and warning signs, allergy/latex avoidance, contraception/teratogenicity counseling when age-appropriate, vaccination planning, lifestyle/physical activity, and uveitis screening importance.
Arrange follow-up for response assessment and lab monitoring (CBC/CMP) after starting methotrexate/NSAID; plan ophthalmology screening for uveitis and school/activity accommodations.
Close with clear return precautions and confirmation of understanding.

acceptable_alternatives: Heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia with established ASCVD

correct_diagnosis.primary: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
required_evidence: Recurrent thunderclap headaches peaking within 1 minute, often over days to weeks
No evidence of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage on initial non-contrast head CT (and/or LP if CT negative and suspicion remains)
Cerebral arterial vasoconstriction on CTA/MRA or catheter angiography (multifocal segmental narrowing/"string of beads")
Reversibility of vasoconstriction on repeat vascular imaging within 12 weeks (or strong clinical suspicion with plan to confirm)
Absence of alternative explanation such as primary angiitis of the CNS (PACNS), aneurysm rupture, cervical artery dissection, or cerebral venous sinus thrombosis

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected unless stress/infection from another cause)
expected_lab_results.BMP - BUN: Typically normal (assess renal function prior to contrast imaging and medication selection)

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/IV every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day if no liver disease; adjust per local policy)
non_pharmacological: Urgent evaluation to exclude secondary causes and complications: non-contrast head CT; if negative and high suspicion for SAH, lumbar puncture; vascular imaging with CTA/MRA (or catheter angiography if needed)
Admit/observe with frequent neurologic checks and blood pressure management (avoid severe hypertension; avoid hypotension)

Identify and stop potential triggers (e.g., triptans, ergot derivatives, cocaine/amphetamines, SSRIs/SNRIs if implicated, cannabis, decongestants, nicotine; postpartum-related counseling as appropriate)
Avoid glucocorticoids unless another diagnosis clearly warrants them (steroids can worsen outcomes in RCVS)
Avoid vasoconstrictive migraine therapies (triptans/ergots) and limit sympathomimetics
Hydration and rest; counsel to avoid intense exertion/sexual activity until stabilized
Screen for complications (ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, convexity SAH, PRES) and treat per standard protocols if present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat CTA/MRA in 6-12 weeks to document reversibility; neurology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge (or sooner if symptoms persist/worsen)

must_include: RCVS is a condition where brain arteries temporarily narrow and usually improve over weeks
Typical symptom is sudden severe (thunderclap) headache; can recur over days to weeks
Need to rule out dangerous mimics like aneurysmal bleeding and other vascular problems
Potential complications include stroke or bleeding, which is why monitoring and imaging are needed
Calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) is used to reduce vasoconstriction and headaches; evidence is mostly observational but commonly recommended
Pain control plan and avoidance of vasoconstrictive headache medicines (triptans/ergots) and sympathomimetics
Plan for repeat vascular imaging in 6-12 weeks to confirm reversibility
Return precautions: new neurologic deficits, persistent/worsening thunderclap headache, seizure, confusion, or severe BP elevation
Discuss expected course: most patients recover, but early period carries risk of stroke/bleeding
Discuss medication side effects (nimodipine can cause low blood pressure, dizziness) and when to seek help
Clarify that steroids are not routinely used for RCVS and may be harmful unless another diagnosis is present
Avoid triggers: stimulants (cocaine/amphetamines), cannabis, nicotine, decongestants, and vasoconstrictive migraine medications
Avoid intense exertion/sexual activity until cleared by neurology
Maintain hydration, sleep, and stress reduction; adhere to follow-up imaging and appointments

Order baseline labs (e.g., CBC, CMP/electrolytes, pregnancy test if applicable, toxicology when indicated) to support safe prescribing (e.g., CCB use), identify secondary causes/triggers, and assess complications.
Review labs to identify red flags (e.g., pregnancy, significant metabolic derangements) and to ensure safe medication selection and disposition.

Order urgent neuroimaging/vascular evaluation to rule out dangerous mimics and assess complications (e.g., non-contrast head CT for SAH, CTA/MRA head/neck; consider LP if CT negative and suspicion for SAH remains; MRI brain if concern for stroke/PRES).
Interpret imaging/LP results to confirm suspected RCVS pattern (multifocal segmental vasoconstriction) and exclude aneurysmal SAH, dissection, venous thrombosis, vasculitis, etc.
Make the working diagnosis of RCVS (or suspected RCVS) after exclusion of emergent mimics and identification of compatible clinical/imaging features.
Perform allergy check prior to prescribing (non-critical but required safety step).
Perform drug interaction check prior to prescribing nimodipine/other meds; critical safety step.

Confirm contraindications prior to prescribing: baseline hypotension, severe aortic stenosis/cardiogenic shock, significant hepatic impairment considerations for CCBs, pregnancy/lactation considerations, and current use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers; also confirm no planned contraindicated orders (critical).

Initiate evidence-consistent therapy: calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) for symptom relief/vasoconstriction, plus a pain control plan; avoid vasoconstrictive agents (triptans/ergots, sympathomimetics).
Communicate diagnosis, treatment plan, expected course, complications, medication side effects, avoidance of triggers, and return precautions per required milestones.
Arrange neurology follow-up and repeat vascular imaging in 6–12 weeks to confirm reversibility and ongoing monitoring.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, management plan, and follow-up/return precautions are complete.

Communicate diagnosis clearly: inherited RBC membrane fragility causing hemolysis; explain anemia and elevated reticulocytes as marrow compensation; discuss subtype differences affecting indices and management; and emphasize hematology confirmation/genetic testing.

Initiate supportive therapy appropriate to chronic hemolysis (e.g., folic acid) and avoid inappropriate therapies (routine iron) unless deficiency proven; transfusion only if severe/symptomatic anemia.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication, and explicitly consider documented penicillin allergy for any future antibiotic needs.

Explain treatment principles: folic acid rationale; transfusion reserved for severe/symptomatic anemia; splenectomy generally avoided due to increased thrombosis risk unless hematology recommends; address patient concerns and inheritance/family implications; provide urgent warning signs.
Provide lifestyle/monitoring education: hydration, individualized trigger avoidance with hematology; avoid unnecessary iron; keep follow-up and periodic CBC/hemolysis monitoring.
Arrange hematology referral/confirmation testing and follow-up interval based on severity; ensure monitoring plan and contingency instructions.
Close encounter after safety-netting, questions answered, and follow-up arranged.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial), episodic
required_evidence: Recurrent attacks of severe, strictly unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes

Clustered pattern over weeks to months with remission periods (or chronic pattern if no remission >3 months)
No red flags suggesting secondary headache (e.g., focal neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, new headache >50, cancer/immunosuppression, thunderclap onset)

Preventive treatment: verapamil to reduce attack frequency; may need dose titration
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper to suppress attacks until preventive works

Discuss verapamil adverse effects (constipation, dizziness, low heart rate) and need for ECG monitoring during titration

Interpret results to risk-stratify (diabetes/insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, hepatic/renal impairment) and determine medication indications/contraindications.
State Prader-Willi-like syndrome due to point mutation, distinguishing it from classic PWS mechanisms, and link diagnosis to clinical phenotype and genetic confirmation.
Explain diagnosis, risks, and prognosis; address stigma; set treatment goals; introduce lifestyle/environmental control and caregiver strategies.
Perform required allergy check before prescribing; confirm penicillin urticarial rash history and document; verify no relevant allergies to proposed metabolic agents.
Perform drug interaction review before prescribing (non-critical but expected) using current medication list and planned agents.
Re-check allergy status immediately prior to finalizing prescriptions if new information emerged during counseling (acceptable redundancy in safety-critical workflows).

Initiate evidence-based pharmacotherapy tailored to results and risks (e.g., GLP-1 RA for appetite/weight/glycemia; metformin for insulin resistance; statin if indicated), including titration and monitoring plan.

If any orders are placed after diagnosis (e.g., sleep study referral orders or additional labs), ensure allergy is not relevant; included for completeness when ordering includes meds/contrast (critical check applies to ordering in this task).

Provide medication counseling: expectations, common adverse effects, red flags (pancreatitis symptoms, dehydration), and what to do if side effects occur; reinforce lifestyle plan and caregiver involvement.
Arrange follow-up to assess tolerability, weight/appetite, glycemic control, and lab monitoring (e.g., 4–12 weeks depending on meds and severity).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly explains that the condition is genetic, causes impaired satiety/hyperphagia, and confers high risk for obesity-related complications.

Agent states that diagnosis is supported by phenotype and confirmed by genetic testing showing a point mutation, and clearly distinguishes this from classic PWS mechanisms (e.g., deletion/UPD/imprinting defects).
Agent reviews key medical risks: type 2 diabetes/insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, fatty liver disease, sleep apnea, and behavioral/psychiatric challenges.
Agent sets treatment goals focused on reducing hyperphagia-driven weight gain, improving metabolic health (glucose, lipids, liver enzymes), and preventing complications.
Agent provides medication rationale and realistic expectations: GLP-1 receptor agonist for appetite/weight and glycemia; metformin for insulin resistance; statin if indicated based on LDL/ASCVD risk.

Agent discusses common adverse effects and red flags: GLP-1 GI effects and dehydration risk; pancreatitis warning symptoms; metformin GI effects and rare lactic acidosis risk in severe renal/hepatic illness; statin myalgias and liver enzyme monitoring.
Agent emphasizes that environmental control/food security (e.g., locked access) is essential because hyperphagia is biologically driven, not a willpower issue.
Agent gives a concrete lifestyle plan: scheduled meals, high-fiber/high-protein options, avoidance of sugar-sweetened beverages, and consistent supervised activity.
Agent explicitly involves caregivers and stresses consistent routines; offers resources/strategies used for PWS-like behavioral management.
Agent acknowledges stigma and validates patient/caregiver concerns, emphasizing biologic basis of appetite dysregulation.
Agent uses shared decision-making addressing feasibility: injections, titration schedule, cost/coverage, and adherence supports.
Agent performs allergy check before prescribing, documents penicillin urticarial rash, and clarifies it does not affect proposed metabolic medications but matters for future antibiotic choices.

Agent performs contraindication checks before ordering and before prescribing; avoids GLP-1 RA in patients with personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN2 and avoids/adjusts metformin in significant renal impairment or severe hepatic illness; avoids statin in pregnancy/active liver disease.
Agent performs drug interaction check before prescribing (even though non-critical) and reconciles current meds.

Agent orders/uses appropriate baseline labs (A1c or fasting glucose, lipid panel, CMP including AST/ALT and creatinine/eGFR; consider urine albumin/creatinine if diabetes) and uses results to justify therapy.
Agent provides follow-up timing and monitoring plan (weight/appetite, A1c/fasting glucose, lipids, liver enzymes as indicated, renal function for metformin).
Agent gives clear return precautions (e.g., severe abdominal pain/vomiting for pancreatitis, signs of dehydration, severe weakness or myalgias with dark urine on statin) and checks understanding.

minimal_paths.description: Standard evaluation and initiation of metabolic/weight management plan for PWS-like syndrome with safety checks and education.
must_collect: dietary environment/food access and caregiver supervision
behavioral/psychiatric concerns (e.g., compulsivity, skin picking, mood)
current medication list and prior weight-loss/diabetes therapies
allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) and adverse drug reactions
contraindication screen for GLP-1 RA (MEN2/MTC history, pancreatitis history) and metformin (renal/hepatic disease, alcohol use) and statin (pregnancy/active liver disease)
steps: ASK: phenotype, hyperphagia, comorbidities, meds, allergies, contraindication screen
ORDER_LAB: A1c/fasting glucose, lipid panel, CMP (AST/ALT, creatinine/eGFR), consider urine ACR if diabetes
GET_RESULTS: determine diabetes/IR, dyslipidemia, NAFLD risk, renal/hepatic suitability
DIAGNOSE: PWS-like syndrome due to point mutation; explain difference from classic PWS
EDUCATE: diagnosis, risks, and stigma; lifestyle/environmental control plan with caregiver involvement
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm/document penicillin urticarial rash; verify no allergies to proposed meds
CHECK_INTERACTION: review interactions with current meds
PRESCRIBE: GLP-1 RA +/- metformin; statin if indicated; include titration and monitoring
EDUCATE: adverse effects/red flags and adherence supports; shared decision-making re injections/cost
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: 4–12 weeks with lab/clinical monitoring plan

acceptable_variants.description: Behavioral and complication-focused variant that additionally prioritizes OSA evaluation and multidisciplinary referrals while still meeting prescribing safety checks.
steps: ASK: emphasize sleep apnea symptoms, behavioral crises, caregiver capacity, food security
ORDER_LAB: metabolic baseline labs; optionally add TSH if clinically indicated, and A1c/lipids/CMP
EDUCATE: diagnosis/risks and structured behavioral/environmental plan; discuss need for sleep study referral
PRESCRIBE: start metformin first if mild obesity/IR and defer GLP-1 RA pending coverage/acceptance; or start GLP-1 RA first if severe hyperphagia/obesity
EDUCATE: medication AEs/red flags and shared decision-making about injections/cost

acceptable_variants.description: Medication-first variant where the agent performs safety checks earlier (before extensive counseling) and then provides education after prescribing, provided all required communication milestones are still completed.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old female administrative assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been exhausted for months and I just don’t feel like myself.” Early in the visit, volunteer: extreme fatigue despite sleep, intermittent dull headaches, weakness with normal chores, unintentional weight gain, and “brain fog”/forgetfulness. If asked about other symptoms, add: feeling cold, dry skin, lightheaded on standing, low appetite, constipation, irregular/lighter or missed periods, low libido, decreased gym strength/muscle loss, morning nausea, and a couple episodes of shakiness/sweating when skipping meals. Do not mention unless directly probed: thinning eyebrows/body hair, poor stress tolerance (crash when sick/busy), emotional flatness/less social interest, hoarse voice, puffy face, and trouble concentrating at work. If asked about sex/fertility, be somewhat hesitant but disclose decreased arousal/orgasm and infertility despite trying. Voice misconceptions: think it’s depression/burnout; suspect thyroid from online reading; worry headaches are migraines. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and open up more. Raise cost concerns about MRIs, endocrinology follow-ups, and multiple meds/labs; ask about spacing visits and OTC alternatives.

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low or low-normal (hyponatremia can occur in secondary adrenal insufficiency due to increased ADH)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal (hyperkalemia is not expected in secondary adrenal insufficiency)

expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Low (consistent with hypotonic hyponatremia if present)

expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (08:00): Low (e.g., clearly below reference range; strongly suggestive if <3–5 µg/dL / <83–138 nmol/L depending on lab)
expected_lab_results.Plasma ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated despite low cortisol)

Start by volunteering: you’ve been really tired and short of breath even walking around the house, feel weak despite full sleep, look pale per family, and your heart races on stairs. If asked about other symptoms, mention: occasional yellow eyes, dark “tea” urine (especially mornings), lightheaded on standing, dull fullness/ache under left ribs after small meals, more headaches, and feeling colder than others. nl_assertions: Agent gathers anemia/hemolysis symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, palpitations, jaundice, dark urine), duration, and severity, and asks about chest pain, syncope, and shortness of breath as red flags.
Agent asks about bleeding symptoms (melena, hematochezia, heavy menses, epistaxis), dietary issues, and family history to avoid anchoring on AIHA prematurely.

Agent asks about recent infections, autoimmune disease history, malignancy/lymphoproliferative symptoms (weight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy), and medication exposures that can precipitate hemolysis.

Agent orders and/or interprets appropriate labs for AIHA evaluation: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, LDH, haptoglobin, total/indirect bilirubin, direct antiglobulin test (DAT/Coombs), type and screen/crossmatch, and basic metabolic panel; considers iron studies and B12/folate if indicated.

Agent correctly interprets typical AIHA pattern (anemia with reticulocytosis, elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin, DAT positive) and acknowledges that reticulocytes may be high because bone marrow is compensating.
Agent communicates diagnosis clearly: immune system attacking red blood cells causing hemolysis and anemia, and emphasizes need for close monitoring because anemia can worsen quickly.

Agent explains treatment: prednisone as first-line to reduce immune destruction of RBCs, expected response timeline (days to 1–2 weeks) and plan to taper once stable, and folic acid to support increased RBC production.

Agent addresses transfusion appropriately: indicates transfusion may be needed for severe/symptomatic anemia and that compatibility testing can be challenging in AIHA; advises coordination with transfusion service and not delaying emergent transfusion when clinically necessary.

Before prescribing, agent completes critical safety checks: confirms/document allergy status (including penicillin allergy described as urticarial rash and clarifies timing/severity) and checks contraindications to planned medications (e.g., uncontrolled infection, severe uncontrolled diabetes, active GI bleeding/ulcer risk considerations).
Agent performs a drug interaction check prior to prescribing (non-critical) and adjusts plan if significant interactions are found.
Agent reviews steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, infection risk, GI upset) and provides clear guidance on when to seek care.
Agent provides red-flag return precautions: chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, rapidly worsening fatigue, dark urine, and fever while on steroids.

Agent provides lifestyle and safety counseling: infection precautions (hand hygiene, prompt evaluation for fever), do not stop prednisone abruptly and follow taper, and adherence to follow-up labs/appointments.

Agent’s plan includes appropriate urgency: recommends ED/urgent evaluation or inpatient management for hemodynamic instability, very low hemoglobin with symptoms, active ischemic symptoms, or rapidly worsening anemia.

minimal_paths.description: Stable patient with lab-confirmed/probable warm AIHA managed initially as outpatient with close follow-up.
steps: ASK: assess symptoms, severity, red flags, bleeding, exposures, comorbidities, and allergy history
ORDER_LAB: CBC, reticulocyte count, smear, LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, DAT, type & screen; consider CMP
GET_RESULTS: confirm hemolysis pattern and DAT positivity; assess hemoglobin severity
DIAGNOSE: probable/confirmed warm AIHA; document rationale and differential
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin allergy details and document
CHECK_INTERACTION: evaluate current meds vs prednisone/folic acid (and any adjuncts)
PRESCRIBE: prednisone + folic acid; provide plan for monitoring and taper after response
EDUCATE: diagnosis, retic explanation, close monitoring, steroid effects, red flags, transfusion considerations, infection precautions, no abrupt stop
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: repeat CBC/hemolysis markers within 48–72 hours to 1 week depending on severity; hematology follow-up

minimal_paths.description: Severe or symptomatic anemia where agent initiates AIHA treatment and escalates for urgent transfusion/inpatient care.
steps: ASK: prioritize red flags, hemodynamic symptoms, bleeding, and timing
ORDER_LAB: CBC, retic, LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, DAT, type & screen/crossmatch; consider troponin/ECG if chest pain
GET_RESULTS: identify severe anemia and hemolysis; DAT positive supports AIHA
PRESCRIBE: start prednisone and folic acid; advise urgent ED/inpatient evaluation; coordinate transfusion planning noting compatibility challenges
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis/treatment, transfusion caveats, urgent return precautions, steroid risks and infection precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: hematology follow-up after stabilization; lab monitoring plan

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders broader secondary cause evaluation concurrently (e.g., ANA, HIV/hepatitis testing, SPEP, CT imaging if malignancy symptoms) while still meeting core AIHA diagnostic and treatment steps.
steps: ORDER_LAB (core hemolysis/DAT + secondary cause labs as indicated)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs safety checks earlier (allergy/contraindication/interaction) immediately before the first prescribing decision, even if diagnosis is stated as 'suspected AIHA' pending DAT.
If transfusion is anticipated, agent adds explicit type-and-screen/crossmatch and communicates that least-incompatible blood may be used when necessary, without delaying life-saving transfusion.

profile.occupation: IT support specialist at a mid-size company

patient.chief_complaint: My hips and lower back have been aching for months, and it’s getting harder to walk for long.

volunteer: My hips and lower back have been aching for months, and it’s getting harder to walk for long.
I feel stiff when I first get up in the morning or after sitting, and it takes a while to loosen up.
I’ve always been shorter than most people, but lately the pain is what’s bothering me.
if_asked: My hips feel tight and I can’t spread my legs as wide as I used to.
Stairs and getting up from a low chair are harder because my hips feel weak and sore.
I sometimes have knee aches after walking a lot.
I’ve noticed my back looks a bit more curved than it used to.
Pain is worse after activity and better with rest; heat helps.
I don’t have fevers or chills.
hidden: I get tired faster than friends when walking because my stride feels short and awkward.
I’ve had on-and-off neck stiffness and headaches, but I didn’t think it mattered.
I’ve had trouble finding clothes that fit because my torso feels shorter compared with my arms and legs.
resistant: I avoid dating and group activities because I’m embarrassed about how I walk and my height.
misleading: I googled it and wondered if it could be ‘inflammation’ like ankylosing spondylitis because my back feels stiff.
I thought it might just be sciatica because sometimes the pain feels like it goes into my buttocks.
noise: My seasonal allergies have been acting up with a runny nose and sneezing.
I’ve had a couple of mild tension headaches from stress at work.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about height history, growth pattern, and family history of similar issues.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked specifically about hip range of motion, functional limits, and daily activities.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about walking distance, gait changes, and exercise tolerance.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about neck symptoms, headaches, and any neurologic warning signs.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I avoid dating and group activities because I’m embarrassed about how I walk and my height.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed only after rapport-building and direct, empathetic questions about mood, social impact, and body image.

general_concerns: Believes his condition is basically a form of osteoporosis and that taking extra calcium/vitamin D will stop the spine and hip problems from progressing.
Assumes that because it is 'dysplasia,' exercise will worsen joint damage and that he should avoid most physical activity rather than doing targeted strengthening and low-impact exercise.
Thinks the autosomal dominant label means each child will definitely inherit the condition (100% risk) rather than a 50% chance per pregnancy.
Believes that if his pain improves with anti-inflammatories, the underlying disease is being treated and will not progress.
Worries that joint replacement is inevitable in the next few years and that there are no effective non-surgical options to delay it.
economic_concerns: Thinks genetic counseling/testing will be extremely expensive and not covered by insurance, so he assumes it is not worth pursuing.
Believes physical therapy is mainly 'optional' and too costly to be useful compared with home workouts, so he expects to skip formal PT.
Assumes that workplace accommodations (ergonomic chair, sit-stand desk, modified duties) will require unpaid leave or risk job loss, so he is hesitant to disclose symptoms at work.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My hips and lower back have been aching for months, and it’s getting harder to walk for long.” Also volunteer: morning/after-sitting stiffness that takes time to loosen, and that you’ve always been shorter than most people.

If asked about details, report: tight hips with reduced leg spread, trouble with stairs/low chairs due to weakness/soreness, occasional knee aches after long walks, slightly more curved back, worse after activity and better with rest; heat helps; no fevers/chills.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: you fatigue faster because your stride feels short/awkward, intermittent neck stiffness/headaches, and difficulty finding clothes because your torso feels short.

Only if rapport/empathy is strong, admit you avoid dating/group activities due to embarrassment about height and gait.

Express misconceptions: worry it might be ankylosing spondylitis (“inflammation”) or sciatica radiating to buttocks. Mention cost fears about genetic testing, skepticism about PT, and anxiety about workplace accommodations risking job loss. If clinician is empathetic, you open up and ask practical, affordable options.

medications: Metformin ER 1000 mg PO twice daily with meals
Empagliflozin 10 mg PO once daily
Lisinopril 10 mg PO once daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Naproxen 250 mg PO twice daily as needed for joint pain (take with food; avoid if dehydrated)
comorbidities: Primary microcephaly-mild intellectual disability-young-onset diabetes syndrome
Chronic mechanical back pain/early osteoarthritis related to skeletal dysplasia
confounders: Early-onset osteoarthritis and chronic mechanical pain can mimic inflammatory arthritis or spondyloarthropathy
Short-trunk disproportionate stature and spinal deformity can complicate BMI interpretation and cardiometabolic risk assessment
Mild intellectual disability may limit symptom reporting and adherence to therapy/physical therapy
Diabetes-related neuropathic pain can overlap with musculoskeletal pain complaints
NSAID use for chronic pain may worsen renal function and confound evaluation of albuminuria
Vitamin D deficiency can contribute to diffuse musculoskeletal pain and weakness, obscuring primary skeletal dysplasia symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia tarda, autosomal dominant
required_evidence: History of disproportionate short stature with short trunk and progressive spine/hip symptoms beginning in late childhood to adolescence
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance (affected individuals across successive generations)
Imaging evidence of platyspondyly and epiphyseal dysplasia (e.g., flattened vertebral bodies, irregular/flattened femoral heads, early hip osteoarthritis)
Absence of inflammatory features suggesting inflammatory spondyloarthropathy (no prolonged morning stiffness, no elevated inflammatory markers if obtained, no sacroiliitis pattern)
No metabolic/endocrine cause of short stature suggested by available data (screening for diabetes may be normal or incidental)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Typically normal unless comorbid diabetes; not diagnostic for SEDT
expected_lab_results.Random plasma glucose: Typically normal unless hyperglycemia is present from another cause
expected_lab_results.Blood urea nitrogen (BUN): Within reference range (important baseline if NSAIDs considered)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed with food (max 1200 mg/day OTC) if no renal disease, GI bleed risk, or uncontrolled hypertension
non_pharmacological: Referral to orthopedics for evaluation of hip/spine degeneration and surgical options if significant functional limitation (e.g., early osteoarthritis, possible joint replacement planning)
Physical therapy focusing on core/hip strengthening, posture, and low-impact aerobic conditioning; avoid high-impact activities that worsen joint pain
Assistive devices as needed (cane, shoe lift if leg-length discrepancy) and activity modification/ergonomics
Genetic counseling for autosomal dominant inheritance, recurrence risk, and family screening options
Consider referral to medical genetics for confirmation and counseling; consider molecular testing if available/appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess pain control and function in 4-6 weeks; sooner (1-2 weeks) if starting NSAID and patient has renal/GI risk factors or symptoms worsen

must_include: Condition is a genetic skeletal dysplasia affecting spine and epiphyses leading to early degenerative joint disease
Autosomal dominant inheritance and implications for family members
Labs listed do not confirm the diagnosis; imaging and clinical history are key
Goals are symptom control, preserving mobility/function, and preventing/mitigating joint degeneration complications
Stepwise pain management (acetaminophen first; NSAID if appropriate) and role of PT/orthopedics
When to consider surgical evaluation (progressive pain, functional limitation, severe hip OA)
Set expectations: chronic condition with variable progression; treatment focuses on quality of life and function
Discuss medication side effects and what to do if adverse effects occur (GI upset/bleeding with NSAIDs; acetaminophen dose limits)
Acknowledge and document allergies (penicillin rash; oxycodone severe nausea/vomiting) and avoid opioid choice that previously caused severe intolerance
Encourage low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling) and weight management to reduce joint load
Joint protection strategies and ergonomics; avoid high-impact activities that exacerbate pain
Importance of follow-up and early reporting of red flags (new neurologic deficits, bowel/bladder changes, rapidly worsening pain)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history: pain location/severity, functional limits, neurologic symptoms/red flags, prior imaging/diagnosis history, family history, current meds/OTC use, pregnancy status if relevant, and prior medication intolerances.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm/document allergies and intolerances (penicillin rash; oxycodone severe nausea/vomiting) and clarify reactions.

Assess contraindications to NSAIDs/acetaminophen (GI bleed/ulcer, kidney disease, cardiovascular risk, anticoagulants, liver disease, alcohol use) and confirm current medication list to support interaction/contraindication screening.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing; identify interactions (e.g., NSAIDs with anticoagulants/antiplatelets, ACEi/ARB+diuretic, SSRIs) and adjust plan accordingly.

Provide stepwise symptom control: acetaminophen first with dose limits; consider NSAID if appropriate after contraindication review; avoid opioid previously causing severe intolerance; consider topical NSAID as alternative; include counseling on side effects and monitoring.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment goals/stepwise plan, expectations, medication risks, lifestyle/low-impact exercise, joint protection, red flags, and family implications of autosomal dominant inheritance.
Arrange follow-up to reassess pain/function, medication tolerability, need for PT/orthopedics, and to review/obtain imaging if not already available; provide earlier follow-up if worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and documenting plan and safety-net instructions.

nl_assertions: Agent explains spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia tarda (autosomal dominant) as a genetic skeletal dysplasia affecting the spine and epiphyses, associated with early degenerative joint disease and chronic pain/functional limitation.

Agent explicitly states that routine labs do not confirm the diagnosis; diagnosis relies on clinical history and characteristic imaging (spine/hip/epiphyseal changes) and may be supported by genetics where available.
Agent discusses autosomal dominant inheritance, including a 50% risk to first-degree relatives, and offers/encourages family counseling and consideration of genetics referral when appropriate.
Agent provides a stepwise pain plan: acetaminophen first-line; NSAID if appropriate after contraindication review; considers topical NSAID and non-pharmacologic measures (PT, activity modification).

Agent identifies clear indications for orthopedic/surgical evaluation: progressive pain, worsening functional limitation, or severe hip osteoarthritis; also mentions spine/neurologic symptoms may warrant urgent evaluation.

Agent completes required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check (critical) and contraindication check (critical); interaction check is performed when feasible and does not delay urgent care if red flags are present.

Agent acknowledges and documents allergies/intolerances (penicillin rash; oxycodone severe nausea/vomiting) and avoids choosing an opioid that previously caused severe intolerance; if analgesic escalation is needed, agent discusses alternatives and risks rather than defaulting to oxycodone.

Agent counsels on medication side effects and actions: NSAID GI upset/ulcer/bleeding, renal risk, CV risk; acetaminophen maximum daily dose and liver toxicity risk; advises to stop and seek care for concerning symptoms (melena, hematemesis, severe abdominal pain, jaundice).
Agent recommends lifestyle measures: low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling), weight management, joint protection/ergonomics, and avoidance of high-impact activities that worsen pain.

Agent provides safety-net instructions for red flags: new neurologic deficits, bowel/bladder changes, saddle anesthesia, rapidly worsening pain, fever/unexplained systemic symptoms, or inability to bear weight.
Agent uses clear, patient-centered language, checks understanding (teach-back), and aligns plan with patient goals and preferences.
Agent does not overpromise cure; sets expectations for a chronic condition with variable progression, emphasizing quality of life and function.
